name: Benu
description: The Benu bird is an ancient Egyptian deity linked to the sun, creation, and rebirth. Often depicted as a heron or a wagtail with long plumes and golden or red plumage, the Benu was believed to be the soul of Ra, the sun god, and was associated with the rising sun, representing renewal and the cycle of life. Some accounts connect the Benu to Atum, the creator god, further emphasizing its role in the origin of the world. The Benu was said to have emerged from the primordial waters of Nun, or from a sacred tree in Heliopolis, a major cult center for the sun god. It was also believed that the Benu renewed itself by fire, much like the phoenix of Greek mythology, and that it was capable of self-creation. Temples dedicated to the Benu were primarily located in Heliopolis, where it was revered as a symbol of hope and resurrection. In funerary contexts, the Benu was associated with the deceased's journey to the afterlife and their eventual rebirth. The Benu's cry was believed to mark the beginning of time, and its presence was seen as a sign of divine favor. Its connection to both the sun and the primordial waters makes it a powerful symbol of cosmic order and the ongoing cycle of creation and destruction.
subtitle: Bird of Creation, Sun, and Rebirth

keyMyths.title: Emergence from the Primordial Waters
keyMyths.description: The Benu bird is said to have arisen from the primordial waters of Nun, symbolizing the beginning of creation and the emergence of life from chaos. This event marks the start of time and the establishment of cosmic order.

keyMyths.title: The Benu and the Sun God Ra
keyMyths.description: The Benu is considered the soul of Ra, the sun god, embodying the daily cycle of the sun's journey across the sky and its eventual rebirth each morning. This association links the Benu to light, warmth, and the continuous renewal of the world.
keyMyths.source: Hymns to Ra and temple inscriptions

keyMyths.title: Renewal Through Fire
keyMyths.description: Similar to the phoenix, the Benu was believed to regenerate itself through fire, symbolizing its ability to overcome death and be reborn. This aspect highlights the Benu's role as a symbol of immortality and the cyclical nature of existence.

sources.author: Geraldine Pinch
sources.work: Egyptian Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Goddesses, and Traditions
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Egyptian mythology, including the Benu bird.
sources.text: Comprehensive overview of Egyptian mythology, including the Benu bird.
